food_subgroup: Grilled chicken skewers served as a plate meal, typically with steamed rice and accompaniments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002764 covers chicken yakitori as a dish concept; the plate/teishoku format with rice is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers grilled chicken meat; the full yakitori plate with rice and tare sauce is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for chicken yakitori plate as a composite dish.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard chicken yakitori plate consisting of approximately 120 g grilled chicken skewers with tare sauce and approximately 200 g steamed white rice, totalling around 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, roasted (USDA SR Legacy 05178) combined with Cooked white rice, short-grain (USDA SR Legacy 20450) and tare sauce component estimated from USDA FoodData Central soy-sauce-based condiment data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite chicken yakitori plate. Values are estimated by weighted combination: approximately 34% grilled chicken thigh (USDA SR Legacy 05178), approximately 57% cooked short-grain white rice (USDA SR Legacy 20450), and approximately 9% tare sauce (soy-sauce-based condiment, USDA FoodData Central). Weighted per-100g values derived from these proportions. Energy cross-checked against Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (Japanese-style, cooked) GI ~54–56
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite chicken yakitori plate. The GI of the plate is dominated by the steamed short-grain white rice component (~57% by weight). Japanese-style cooked short-grain white rice has a GI of approximately 54–56 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The protein and fat from the chicken and tare sauce lower the effective GI slightly. A composite GI of 54 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (54 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 46.3, rounded to 47.0. Carbohydrate per serving = 24.5 g/100g × 350 g = 85.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tare sauce (soy sauce base)
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing over 47% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium tare sauce or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from steamed white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load per serving is approximately 47, driven by the white rice base; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, substituting with brown rice, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Relatively low saturated fat with moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content is modest at approximately 3.2 g per 100 g, making this a lower-risk choice for cardiovascular disease compared to fried or heavily marbled meat dishes, though the sodium load from tare sauce warrants attention for those managing blood pressure as a cardiovascular risk factor.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Yakitori Plate (鶏の焼き鳥プレート) is a Japanese grilled chicken dish served as a complete plate meal. Yakitori refers to bite-sized pieces of chicken — typically thigh meat for its juiciness — threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal or a gas grill while being repeatedly basted with tare, a sweet-savoury glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The result is tender, slightly caramelised chicken with a smoky, umami-rich flavour and a lightly sticky exterior. In a plate format (teishoku style), the skewers are served alongside a generous portion of steamed short-grain white rice and may include pickled vegetables or miso soup. Nutritionally, the plate provides moderate protein (~34 g per serving) from the chicken, a significant carbohydrate load from the rice (~86 g per serving), and relatively low saturated fat. The sodium content is elevated due to the soy-sauce-based tare glaze, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load is moderate-to-high per serving owing to the white rice, which is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Chicken yakitori is a widely popular street food and restaurant staple in Japan and is commonly found in Japanese restaurant chains across Singapore, including at Sukiya outlets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a yakitori plate?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite chicken yakitori plate as served in a Singapore restaurant context. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy entries for grilled chicken thigh, cooked short-grain white rice, and a soy-sauce-based condiment. GI is an analogous estimate based on the rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy components (grilled chicken thigh ~34%, cooked short-grain white rice ~57%, tare sauce ~9%) as no single approved-source composite entry exists for this dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this estimation. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient reliability across the component sources to produce a defensible composite estimate. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked short-grain white rice used in this estimation. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the chicken and rice components — naturally zero in these minimally processed ingredients. 6. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese-style cooked short-grain white rice per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); the chicken and tare components do not contribute meaningfully to glycemic response. 7. The input cuisine_tags included 'Beef Dish' and 'Japanese Curry', which appear to be erroneous tags from the field survey platform aggregation for the Sukiya restaurant; these have been disregarded as they do not describe this dish. 8. Halal status is marked false as Sukiya Singapore uses pork-derived ingredients in some products and the halal certification status of this specific item could not be confirmed from approved sources.
